title: Operator Resilience Safety-net and Playbook
preview:
Summary
This playbook codifies operational safety-nets and a lightweight runbook lifecycle engineered to reduce operator fatigue while preserving rapid restoration for Redwood Inference services. It combines: 1) pragmatic runbook trimming, 2) safety-net automation, 3) a synthetic incident lab for continuous readines...
text:
Summary
This playbook codifies operational safety-nets and a lightweight runbook lifecycle engineered to reduce operator fatigue while preserving rapid restoration for Redwood Inference services. It combines: 1) pragmatic runbook trimming, 2) safety-net automation, 3) a synthetic incident lab for continuous readiness, and 4) customer-facing status templates.

When to use this playbook
- Primary: multi-region latency regressions, cache-thrashing events, and burst-rate capacity exhaustion where immediate bypass or temporary mitigation is required.
- Secondary: learning exercises and runbook pruning sprints.

Scope and goals
- Restore service within SLO windows: P99 latency < 250ms for inference proxies; request success >= 99.5% over 5m.
- Reduce mean time-to-diagnose (MTTD) by 30% through automated evidence capture.
- Limit oncall cognitive load: surface 3 prioritized actions per runboo...

title: Postmortem: Dedicated capacity starvation on gpu-pool-delta (2026-03-10)
preview:
## Summary
On 2026-03-10, several Dedicated customers experienced elevated queueing delays and request timeouts due to capacity starvation in gpu-pool-delta. The pool had sufficient total GPUs, but an imbalance in GPU fragmentation and incorrect priority weights prevented timely scheduling of certain large-model rep...
text:
## Summary
On 2026-03-10, several Dedicated customers experienced elevated queueing delays and request timeouts due to capacity starvation in gpu-pool-delta. The pool had sufficient total GPUs, but an imbalance in GPU fragmentation and incorrect priority weights prevented timely scheduling of certain large-model replicas.

## Customer impact
- Duration: 1 hour 26 minutes
- Symptoms: increased queue depth; p95 latency +55%; intermittent 504s
- Scope: Dedicated customers mapped to gpu-pool-delta in us-east-1

## Detection
- Alert: dedicated queue depth > threshold
- Customer Success escalation: "timeouts on pinned dedicated endpoint"

## Timeline (UTC)
- 17:42: Queue depth alerts fire
- 17:55: Infra on-call identifies fragmentation + weight misconfig
- 18:10: Mitigation: move two tenants to gpu-pool-echo; restart scheduler with corrected weights
- 19:08: Queue depth returns to normal

#...

title: Operational Rotation Capacity and Customer Status Playbook
preview:
Overview:

This playbook defines how Redwood scales on-call capacity against SLOs, how we choreograph rapid escalations, and the exact cadence + templates for customer-facing status updates during multi-tenant incidents. text:
Overview:

This playbook defines how Redwood scales on-call capacity against SLOs, how we choreograph rapid escalations, and the exact cadence + templates for customer-facing status updates during multi-tenant incidents. It is intended for SREs, on-call engineers, product stewards, and customer-facing support teams.

Goals:
- Ensure incidents are handled within SLO-driven timeboxes (see SLO section) with predictable staffing cost.
- Provide a standard escalation choreography that minimizes firefighting and accelerates decisions.
- Provide clear, templated, and consistent customer communications for each incident class.
- Capture evidence and postmortem artifacts to close the learning loop.

Scope:
- Applies to all Redwood production-facing inference services (hosted API, Dedicated control plane, and Private control-plane endpoints).
- Does NOT replace service-level runbooks; it augmen...

title: P1 Incident Postmortem: Private deployments upgrade regression (auth handshake failures)
preview:
## Summary
On 2026-02-27, a subset of Private (VPC) deployments experienced intermittent auth handshake failures after upgrading the private gateway. The issue was resolved by rolling back a TLS library change and issuing a patched build.

## Severity
P1

## Customer impact

text:
## Summary
On 2026-02-27, a subset of Private (VPC) deployments experienced intermittent auth handshake failures after upgrading the private gateway. The issue was resolved by rolling back a TLS library change and issuing a patched build.

## Severity
P1

## Customer impact
- 4 Private deployments saw 401/handshake errors for ~41 minutes.
- Hosted API was not impacted.

## Timeline (UTC)
- 21:02: First report from customer SE channel.
- 21:10: Engineering engaged; suspect gateway upgrade.
- 21:24: Rollback initiated for affected deployments.
- 21:43: Error rate returns to baseline.

## Root cause
A TLS renegotiation setting changed behavior for clients using older OpenSSL versions, causing the gateway to terminate connections during token exchange.

## Detection
Detected via customer reports; existing dashboard did not separate handshake failures by client TLS fingerprint.

## Follow-...

title: P1 Incident Postmortem: Metrics pipeline dropped tracing spans
preview:
## Summary
On 2026-02-25, Redwood Console traces for several services showed missing spans and incomplete request graphs. The underlying services were healthy, but the observability pipeline dropped a portion of spans under burst load.

## Severity
P1

## Customer impact
- Internal impact: on-call and support debugg...
text:
## Summary
On 2026-02-25, Redwood Console traces for several services showed missing spans and incomplete request graphs. The underlying services were healthy, but the observability pipeline dropped a portion of spans under burst load.

## Severity
P1

## Customer impact
- Internal impact: on-call and support debugging slowed due to partial traces for ~2 hours.
- No direct serving impact; data plane SLOs were met.

## Timeline (UTC)
- 12:08: First report from on-call: trace graphs missing child spans.
- 12:22: SRE identifies span-ingester backpressure and queue drops.
- 12:44: Mitigation: increase ingester replicas + raise queue limits temporarily.
- 14:05: Drop rate returns to baseline; backlog drains.

## Root cause
The span ingester queue was configured with a low max size and a drop-on-full policy. A parallel deploy increased span volume per request (additional attributes), pushin...

title: Postmortem: 2026-02-17 Rollout latency regression — auto-rollback did not trigger due to missing/stale monitors
preview:
## Summary
On 2026-02-17 we ran a controlled rollout (internal production route) of a new model serving profile for `redwood-api/chat-gateway` in `us-east-1` (Self-serve tier), moving traffic from **model version `rw-mistral-7b-instruct-2026.02.10`** (baseline) to **`rw-mistral-7b-instruct-2026.02.17`** (candidate)....
text:
## Summary
On 2026-02-17 we ran a controlled rollout (internal production route) of a new model serving profile for `redwood-api/chat-gateway` in `us-east-1` (Self-serve tier), moving traffic from **model version `rw-mistral-7b-instruct-2026.02.10`** (baseline) to **`rw-mistral-7b-instruct-2026.02.17`** (candidate).

During the canary step, the candidate experienced a **p99 latency regression** (tail latency increase) and a brief **5xx spike** under bursty traffic. We expected the rollout controller to auto-pause/auto-rollback based on the configured SLO monitors. No automated action occurred.

The on-call engineer manually paused the rollout after observing the degradation in Grafana. Impact was limited due to low canary percentage and quick manual mitigation, but the event revealed a critical gap: **the SLO monitor query used by the controller returned missing/stale data for the rol...

Summary:

This document defines an operator action grid that maps common detection signals to immediate runbook steps, time-to-recovery (TTR) targets, and a staged customer notification strategy. It includes ready-to-use status-page JSON, Tiered customer notice scripts, and short game-day exercises to validate operator speed and communication clarity. Scope and intent:

- Scope: Redwood inference runtime components (request routing, KV cache, batching layer, model variants), API gateway, and customer-facing rate limiting and quota subsystems.

## Severity

text:
## Summary
On 2026-02-20, one dedicated customer cluster experienced a routing loop between the edge router and the regional policy router after a policy update. This caused request amplification, elevated tail latency, and intermittent 503s for ~19 minutes until policy rollback.

## Severity
P0

## Customer impact
- Impacted: 1 dedicated customer environment in us-west (production).
- Symptoms: p99 latency increased from ~1.2s to > 18s; peak 503 rate 11%.

## Timeline (UTC)
- 05:14: Policy update applied (new fallback rule).
- 05:18: Latency alerts fire; on-call engaged.
- 05:25: Suspected loop; rollback initiated.
- 05:33: Metrics normalize.

## Root cause
A misconfigured fallback route allowed traffic to be re-ingested by the edge router with no loop detection. A recent refactor removed the hop-count header from dedicated routing.

## Detection
Detected via dedicated SLO alerting (...

title: Dependency Failure Containment and Customer Communication Cookbook
preview:
Summary:

It focuses on rapid containment patterns, safe degradations, and customer-facing communi...
text:
Summary:
This cookbook describes standard procedures for containing, mitigating, and communicating incidents caused by external dependencies (third-party APIs, cloud managed services, vendor models, and shared infra components). It focuses on rapid containment patterns, safe degradations, and customer-facing communications. The goal is fast, predictable response with clear ownership and minimal customer surprise.

Scope:
- Applies to incidents where a Redwood customer-visible failure is caused primarily by a non-Redwood dependency (e.g., upstream model host, cloud DB region, vendor auth provider, CDN outage).
- Does NOT replace generic service outage runbooks; it augments them with dependency-specific checks, routing options, and communication templates.
- Intended audience: on-call SREs, service owners, incident commanders, and customer success leads.

TL;DR Playbook (one-page):
1) D...

Overview

This page is the canonical library for: (A) structured incident status timelines used by Redwood Inference responder teams and (B) the automation specification for generating and publishing those timelines to the status page, internal Slack bridge, and customer notification channels. Who should use this

- Customer Success for pre-approved messaging when high-impact incidents occur

Principles

1) Single source of truth: the status timeline generator (STG) must accept a single incident payload (incident_id) and emit consistent updates to all channels.

title: Rollout Incident Playbook: Auto-mitigation (SLO-triggered pause/rollback)
preview:
## Purpose
This playbook describes how on-call responders should use Redwood’s rollout auto-mitigation (SLO-triggered **pause** and/or **rollback**) during incidents, including when to trust automation vs take manual action, communication expectations, and escalation paths if auto-mitigation fails or worsens the sit...
text:
## Purpose
This playbook describes how on-call responders should use Redwood’s rollout auto-mitigation (SLO-triggered **pause** and/or **rollback**) during incidents, including when to trust automation vs take manual action, communication expectations, and escalation paths if auto-mitigation fails or worsens the situation.

This is an incident response supplement to the deeper, step-by-step operational runbook:
- See: **Auto-rollback on SLO breach runbook** (related page)

---

## Scope
Applies to incidents involving:
- Canary rollouts, staged deployments, or A/B traffic splits for **model versions** and serving routes.
- Automated safety actions driven by **latency/error-rate/saturation** SLO monitors at a defined scope:
 - route/service, model version, region, customer tier

Out of scope:
- Multi-region failover orchestration (beyond pause/rollback)
- Quality/regression-eval-driven ...

title: Postmortem: Rate limit misconfiguration caused elevated 429s (2026-02-14)
preview:
## Summary
On 2026-02-14 a configuration change to the API gateway introduced stricter per-minute limits for several customer tiers. This led to a spike in HTTP 429 responses for otherwise healthy traffic patterns.

## Customer impact
- Duration: 47 minutes
- Symptoms: increased 429s; downstream retries amplified tr...
text:
## Summary
On 2026-02-14 a configuration change to the API gateway introduced stricter per-minute limits for several customer tiers. This led to a spike in HTTP 429 responses for otherwise healthy traffic patterns.

## Customer impact
- Duration: 47 minutes
- Symptoms: increased 429s; downstream retries amplified traffic
- Scope: Primarily self-serve customers on Hosted API

## Detection
- Alert: 429 rate anomaly at API gateway
- Support: multiple reports of sudden throttling

## Timeline (UTC)
- 21:03: 429 anomaly alert fired
- 21:10: On-call identifies config rollout to gateway
- 21:22: Mitigation: revert gateway config bundle
- 21:50: 429 rates return to baseline

## Root cause
A new tier mapping table was deployed with a default value that applied an enterprise limit to the wrong tier group. The change passed review but lacked an automated validation against known tier fixtures.

...

title: Postmortem: Perf Canary False-Positive Alert Storm (baseline drift + cardinality)
preview:
## Summary
On 2026-02-12, the production performance canary generated a burst of false-positive alerts across multiple models and regions shortly after enabling warning + paging rules in the first expansion beyond the initial 2-model rollout. Alerts were high volume (warning + paging) and repeatedly reopened due to ...
text:
## Summary
On 2026-02-12, the production performance canary generated a burst of false-positive alerts across multiple models and regions shortly after enabling warning + paging rules in the first expansion beyond the initial 2-model rollout. Alerts were high volume (warning + paging) and repeatedly reopened due to dedupe failures, resulting in on-call interruption and a temporary release freeze request.

There was **no confirmed customer-facing latency regression** attributable to this incident. The event was an alerting/observability failure caused primarily by (1) **baseline drift + baseline key mismatch** and (2) **metric label cardinality inflation** that fragmented the canary’s time series, defeating min-sample gating and dedupe.

## Impact
### Customer impact
- **None confirmed.** Runtime and customer SLO dashboards did not show correlated sustained p95 latency increases for cu...

title: Error Budget Breach → Incident Process (Hosted + Dedicated)
preview:
## Purpose
This page defines **when an error budget burn becomes an incident** and standardizes the **required roles, timelines, and update expectations** for Redwood Inference’s tiered SLO program.

The goal is to eliminate ambiguity during reliability events (especially burst traffic events causing latency spikes,...
text:
## Purpose
This page defines **when an error budget burn becomes an incident** and standardizes the **required roles, timelines, and update expectations** for Redwood Inference’s tiered SLO program.

The goal is to eliminate ambiguity during reliability events (especially burst traffic events causing latency spikes, 5xx, and streaming disconnects) and to ensure that Product and Customer Success can align customer communication to measurable engineering signals.

This process applies to **Hosted (shared)** and **Dedicated (reserved capacity)** tiers. (Private is out of scope for enforcement in this phase.)

---

## Definitions (quick)
- **SLI**: Service Level Indicator (measured metric). In scope: request success rate (non-5xx), end-to-end latency percentiles, and streaming reliability (disconnect/error events) per tier/region/endpoint class.
- **SLO**: Target for an SLI over a defined...

title: Tiered Status Communications Guidelines (Hosted vs Dedicated)
preview:
## Purpose
This page defines how Redwood communicates incidents and reliability events when impact differs by **tier**:
- **Hosted (shared)**: multi-tenant API (self-serve + paid plans).
- **Dedicated (reserved capacity)**: customer-isolated capacity with tier-specific operational commitments.

It provides:
- Criter...
text:
## Purpose
This page defines how Redwood communicates incidents and reliability events when impact differs by **tier**:
- **Hosted (shared)**: multi-tenant API (self-serve + paid plans).
- **Dedicated (reserved capacity)**: customer-isolated capacity with tier-specific operational commitments.

It provides:
- Criteria for publishing to the **public status page** vs **tier-scoped comms** (Dedicated-only notifications, account manager outreach).
- Standard wording templates for each tier.
- Cadences and required fields for updates.

This guidance is aligned to the tiered SLO/error budget program. We aim to be precise, consistent, and avoid over/under-communicating impact.

---
## Principles
1) **Be explicit about who is impacted** (Hosted vs Dedicated; which regions; which endpoints).
2) **Do not infer impact**: only state what we can validate via dashboards/telemetry.
3) **Avoid custom...

Overview

Purpose: This document defines the escalation cadence, a standardized playbook for high-severity (blitz) incidents affecting customer-facing inference throughput or correctness, and the templates we use for runbooks, status page updates, and customer communications. It is intended to reduce time-to-restoration, clarify responsibilities, and ensure consistent post-incident learning.

Scope: Applies to the Redwood inference control plane, hosted API endpoints, Dedicated gateway agents, and cache/kv subsystems. Do NOT use this playbook for purely billing/metadata issues or non-production sandbox incidents.

Quick links
- Incident tracking channel: #prod-incidents (Slack)
- PagerDuty: Redwood Prod Escalation
- Status page template: /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/status-page-templates

Definitions and severity matrix

We use three operational severities for production i...

- **Support customer comms** (email + ticket updates)

text:
## Purpose
This page defines how Redwood Inference communicates during customer-impacting incidents across:
- **Support customer comms** (email + ticket updates)
- **Slack bridges** (#support bridge channels and #incidents)
- **Status page updates** (public and/or customer-facing status events)

It provides:
1) A shared definition of what qualifies for a **status page incident**
2) A single **approval flow** so customers receive consistent, accurate updates
3) A standard **message structure** so Support, SRE, and Platform are aligned on impact, mitigations, and timelines

This is written to reduce rework, avoid conflicting statements, and keep our comms aligned with on-call practices and customer contract expectations.

---

## Scope and non-goals

- Customer-impacting incidents and degradations across Hosted API, Dedicated, and Private (when Redwood is responsible for a s...

title: P0 Incident Postmortem: Control plane API outage during config rollout
preview:
## Summary
On 2026-02-07, the control plane API returned elevated 5xx and became unavailable for ~14 minutes. The data plane continued serving most requests, but customers could not create keys, change routing policies, or update deployments.

## Severity
P0

## Customer impact
- Control plane unavailable for 14 min...
text:
## Summary
On 2026-02-07, the control plane API returned elevated 5xx and became unavailable for ~14 minutes. The data plane continued serving most requests, but customers could not create keys, change routing policies, or update deployments.

## Severity
P0

## Customer impact
- Control plane unavailable for 14 minutes (peak error rate 82%).
- Console actions failed; CI/CD integrations relying on control plane were blocked.

## Timeline (UTC)
- 09:42: Automated rollout begins (config schema update).
- 09:47: 5xx spikes; autoscaler adds pods but errors persist.
- 09:52: Rollout paused; manual rollback attempted.
- 09:56: Traffic restored after reverting schema + clearing stale caches.

## Root cause
A new config schema field triggered an unbounded cache key cardinality in the policy evaluator service. Under normal read/write load, memory pressure caused repeated OOM kills and cascadin...

title: Holiday & Maintenance Window On-call Orchestration and Customer Communication Toolkit

It covers on-call rotation adaptations, a concise operational runbook for scheduled degradations, pre- and post-maintenance game-day exercises, and customer-facing status templates. ...
text:
Overview

Purpose: This document standardizes how Redwood operates scheduled maintenance and holiday windows when staffing is reduced. It covers on-call rotation adaptations, a concise operational runbook for scheduled degradations, pre- and post-maintenance game-day exercises, and customer-facing status templates. The goal is to maintain SLOs where possible, minimize customer surprise, and ensure a clear, timeboxed recovery path if regressions occur.

Scope
- Services in-scope: redwood-serving, routing-proxy, kv-cache, console-api, billing-queue, telemetry-ingest.
- Applies to: company-approved maintenance (deploys, infra upgrades, region maintenance) and planned holiday staffing reductions (global public holidays, end-of-year window).

Key principles
- Prioritize availability-critical routes: routing-proxy and redwood-serving production routes must be treated with highest priority.
...

title: Game Day Plan: Hot-Route Overload Simulation (Hosted API)
preview:
## Purpose
This game day validates that **hot-route capacity protection** prevents fleet-wide degradation when a single high-traffic route experiences a burst. We will intentionally overload a protected route (initially `/v1/chat/completions`) and verify:
- Enterprise-priority traffic preserves defined latency/avail...
text:
## Purpose
This game day validates that **hot-route capacity protection** prevents fleet-wide degradation when a single high-traffic route experiences a burst. We will intentionally overload a protected route (initially `/v1/chat/completions`) and verify:
- Enterprise-priority traffic preserves defined latency/availability behavior.
- Best-effort traffic is throttled/shed earlier with consistent, explainable 429 semantics.
- Routing/admission control responds to real capacity signals (queue depth/scheduler lag) without causing cascading failure.
- Observability, alerting, and on-call procedures are usable under pressure.

This is **not** a chaos test of the entire fleet. It is a controlled exercise with strict stop conditions.

---

## Scope
### In scope
- Hosted API (and Dedicated shared routing surfaces, where applicable) for one region at a time.
- Tier-aware rate limiting + quota ...

title: Regional Failover Communication Playbook (Hosted API + Dedicated)
preview:
## Purpose
This playbook standardizes *who says what, where, and when* during a **regional impairment** or **regional failover** affecting Redwood Inference’s Hosted API and/or Dedicated capacity.

It is designed for:
- **SRE / Incident Commander (IC):** to drive internal coordination and ensure externally visible u...
text:
## Purpose
This playbook standardizes *who says what, where, and when* during a **regional impairment** or **regional failover** affecting Redwood Inference’s Hosted API and/or Dedicated capacity.

It is designed for:
- **SRE / Incident Commander (IC):** to drive internal coordination and ensure externally visible updates are consistent and timely.
- **Support / CS (Customer Success):** to respond to inbound questions with approved language and the right level of detail.
- **Product / Comms approvers:** to review customer-facing statements (status page + proactive outreach) for clarity and promise discipline.

## Scope
**In-scope events**
- Any *suspected or confirmed* regional impairment (e.g., elevated 5xx, sustained tail latency burn, network reachability failures) that may trigger or has triggered **traffic steering / failover**.
- **Automation-driven failover actions** (including...

title: Postmortem: 2026-01-18 Hosted Latency Regression Causing Rapid Error Budget Burn
preview:
## Summary
On 2026-01-18, Redwood Hosted (shared) experienced a latency regression on the chat/completions request path after a runtime configuration change and a routing preference change were deployed within the same hour. The combined effect increased queueing and tail latency, triggering downstream timeouts and ...
text:
## Summary
On 2026-01-18, Redwood Hosted (shared) experienced a latency regression on the chat/completions request path after a runtime configuration change and a routing preference change were deployed within the same hour. The combined effect increased queueing and tail latency, triggering downstream timeouts and elevated 5xx rates for a subset of traffic.

The incident led to rapid **error budget burn** for Hosted and exposed gaps in our ability to (a) detect budget burn early, (b) prevent risky rollouts when reliability is degraded, and (c) clearly separate Hosted vs Dedicated accounting during mitigation actions (fallbacks/traffic shifting).

**Primary impact:** tail latency and intermittent 5xx for Hosted chat/completions (including streaming disconnects). **Dedicated** was not the target, but mitigation actions (traffic shifting) created brief contention signals in the shared c...

title: Postmortem: Regional failover routed to incompatible model variants (2026-02-03)
preview:
## Summary
On 2026-02-03 a transient network event degraded availability in eu-west-1. Automated failover shifted a portion of traffic to us-east-1, but the routing policy selected model variants that were API-compatible yet behaviorally incompatible for a subset of customers who had pinned specific model families.

text:
## Summary
On 2026-02-03 a transient network event degraded availability in eu-west-1. Automated failover shifted a portion of traffic to us-east-1, but the routing policy selected model variants that were API-compatible yet behaviorally incompatible for a subset of customers who had pinned specific model families.

## Customer impact
- Duration: 33 minutes
- Symptoms: elevated 4xx due to validation mismatches, and quality complaints due to unexpected model behavior
- Scope: ~6% of eu-west-1 traffic during failover window

## Detection
- Alert: eu-west-1 error-rate SLO burn
- Support tickets flagged "model changed during failover"

## Timeline (UTC)
- 08:22: Network packet loss observed in eu-west-1
- 08:24: Failover begins
- 08:31: Increase in 4xx and customer complaints
- 08:44: Routing policy patched to restrict eligible variants
- 08:55: Incident resolved

## Root cause
The failov...

title: P1 Incident Postmortem: Streaming responses intermittently hang
preview:
## Summary
On 2026-02-03, some customers reported that streaming chat completions would start normally and then stop producing tokens. Requests remained open until client-side timeouts. The incident impacted hosted API streaming routes for ~27 minutes.

## Severity
P1

## Customer impact
- ~3.1% of streaming request...
text:
## Summary
On 2026-02-03, some customers reported that streaming chat completions would start normally and then stop producing tokens. Requests remained open until client-side timeouts. The incident impacted hosted API streaming routes for ~27 minutes.

## Severity
P1

## Customer impact
- ~3.1% of streaming requests in us-east experienced a stall > 30s.
- Non-streaming requests were not affected.

## Timeline (UTC)
- 18:06: Incident starts (increased reports, elevated open connections).
- 18:11: On-call acknowledges; partial mitigation by lowering max concurrent streams per pod.
- 18:20: Hotfix: disable experimental backpressure setting in the stream proxy.
- 18:33: Metrics return to baseline.

## Root cause
A recent runtime upgrade changed the stream chunk flush behavior, which interacted badly with the gateway's backpressure configuration. Under specific token burst patterns, the s...

title: Rapid Stabilization Cadre: Protocols and Template Kit
preview:
Overview

This page documents the 'Rapid Stabilization Cadre' (RSC) pattern: a compact, cross-functional responder unit that executes a time-boxed containment sprint for high-severity incidents affecting Redwood Inference’s serving surface. text:
Overview

This page documents the 'Rapid Stabilization Cadre' (RSC) pattern: a compact, cross-functional responder unit that executes a time-boxed containment sprint for high-severity incidents affecting Redwood Inference’s serving surface. The kit includes invocation criteria, a three-phase sprint checklist, handoff protocols for on-call rotations, a postmortem rubric tailored to sprint incidents, a short game-day syllabus, and ready-to-send status page / customer comms templates.

Goals
- Restore acceptable service levels within the first 60 minutes for Sev-1 and within 3 hours for complicated multi-region outages.
- Contain customer impact and preserve forensic evidence for later RCA.
- Produce a clear handoff artifact and postmortem within defined SLAs.

Scope
- Applies to incidents impacting production inference latency SLOs (>25% of requests > 2x latency SLO), availability SLOs ...

Summary
-------
This document defines an SLO-driven incident response pattern we call the "fallthrough flow": automated detection of SLO boundary crossings, progressive mitigation (automated routing and model fallbacks), and human-run escalation/playbooks where automation cannot resolve the issue. It combines end-to-end runbooks, on-call rotation guidance, game day scenarios, status page and customer communications templates, and a postmortem checklist.

Scope
-----
- Services: model-serving (hosted and dedicated), routing layer, cache services (prefix/KV), and the control plane that performs routing and rollouts.
- Intended for incidents that originate from performance or availability regressions that cause SLO boundary crossings (latency, request error rate, embedding failure rate).
- Not intended for security incidents (see security-and-compliance incident playbooks).

Summary:

This cookbook documents the standard process for on-call handovers, structured shadowing, and incident escalation at Redwood Inference. It consolidates the operational steps, checklists, templates, and example runbook fragments teams should adopt to reduce knowledge gaps during rotation changes, accelerate TTR, and ensure consistent customer communications.

Scope and applicability:
- Applies to all engineering teams participating in the platform on-call rotation (Serving Runtime, SRE, Platform, and Private Deployments support sub-teams).
- Covers: shift handovers, 24–72 hour shadowing plans for new responders, live incident escalation paths, internal and external communication templates, and post-incident handoff into remediation work.

Goals:
- Minimize context loss between shifts and during escalations.

Overview:

This document is a consolidated contingency catalog for common oncall scenarios plus a structured onboarding checklist for new responders and rotation leads. It is intended to reduce decision overhead during incidents, accelerate time-to-containment, and standardize customer and status-page communications. The content below includes classification guidance, runbook snippets for high-frequency faults (example: degraded KV cache), an onboarding checklist for rotation members, a pre-baked customer/status communication kit, and a lightweight post-incident learning cadence.

Scope and audience:

- Scope: Redwood inference production services (hosted API, dedicated routing, private deployment control plane) and the infra & data-plane components that most commonly affect customer-facing latency and availability.

Summary:
This playbook describes the activation, composition, and operating procedures for Redwood’s Swift Response Taskforce (SRT). It standardizes rapid stabilization steps, evidence collection, CSR (customer success representative) brief scripts, and post-incident handoffs for incidents that risk violating customer-facing SLOs or require immediate customer outreach. Use this document when an incident meets Severity 1 or a high-severity multi-tenant degradation (see \"Severity and Activation Matrix\").

Purpose and scope:
- Provide a lightweight, reproducible pattern for assembling a cross-functional rapid-response team within the first 15 minutes of detection.
- Ensure consistent, brand-safe customer messaging from first detection through postmortem.
- Define measurable stabilization objectives, evidence artifacts, and closure criteria.

title: Responder wellbeing and orchestration guide
preview:
Overview

Purpose: This guide unifies three practical priorities for Redwood responders: (1) a compact incident lifecycle focused on fast containment and humane workload, (2) repeatable runbook patterns and postmortem scaffolding, and (3) on-call rotation and game-day design that preserves responder wellbeing while ...
text:
Overview

Purpose: This guide unifies three practical priorities for Redwood responders: (1) a compact incident lifecycle focused on fast containment and humane workload, (2) repeatable runbook patterns and postmortem scaffolding, and (3) on-call rotation and game-day design that preserves responder wellbeing while meeting SLO obligations.

Scope: applies to all teams that operate inference services (api-frontend, inference-router, kv-cache, model-host). This is an operational reference — for team-specific runbooks link to the service page.

Owner and escalation

- Primary owner: Eng SRE (pager rotation managed in OnCall roster).
- Secondary owner: team that owns the impacted service (see escalation matrix below).
- Pager escalation: primary -> secondary -> SRE lead -> CTO on extended incidents (>4 hours).

title: Incident Classification Policy (incident_type + owner required)
preview:
## Purpose
This policy defines when and how incidents must be classified using the **Incident Taxonomy v1** (incident_type + subtype), who is responsible for setting/updating classification during an incident, how to handle “unknown” classifications, and the enforcement rules that prevent closing incidents without r...
text:
## Purpose
This policy defines when and how incidents must be classified using the **Incident Taxonomy v1** (incident_type + subtype), who is responsible for setting/updating classification during an incident, how to handle “unknown” classifications, and the enforcement rules that prevent closing incidents without required fields.

Classification is required so we can:
- Route mitigation work to the correct owning team quickly.
- Track incident volume and impact by type/owner.
- Measure remediation SLA compliance by severity and customer tier.
- Reduce recurring incidents through clear accountability.

## Scope
This policy applies to all incidents created via incident-bot and tracked in our incident tooling, including:
- Customer-impacting incidents (Hosted API, Dedicated, Private).
- High-severity internal incidents that threaten reliability (even if impact is not yet confirmed).

Th...

title: Restoration Orchestra Playbook for LLM Serving Epsilon Events
preview:
Summary
This playbook covers fast, repeatable response patterns for small-to-medium ("epsilon") degradations of Redwood's LLM serving surface caused by short-lived traffic shifts, KV cache entropy, noisy neighbors, or routing oscillations. These events typically cause transient P99 latency spikes, elevated tail erro...
text:
Summary
This playbook covers fast, repeatable response patterns for small-to-medium ("epsilon") degradations of Redwood's LLM serving surface caused by short-lived traffic shifts, KV cache entropy, noisy neighbors, or routing oscillations. These events typically cause transient P99 latency spikes, elevated tail error rates, and benign customer impact if left unhandled. The goal: contain impact in 10-30 minutes and restore steady-state without a full rollback.

Scope and when to use this playbook
- Use for incidents classified Severity 2 (degraded performance with partial customer impact) and high-priority S3 transient patterns that show clear signal but no data loss.
- Do NOT use for full regional outages, sustained model quality regressions, or security incidents; follow the outage and security runbooks instead.

Key concepts and roles
- Conductor (oncall engineer): coordinates the r...

text:
Overview
========

Purpose: This page codifies a repeatable, SLO-driven approach for on‑call transitions, escalation ladders, and a blameless postmortem cadence. It is designed for engineers, SREs, and on‑call leads who perform handoffs, run game days, and own incident learning loops.

Scope: applies to Redwood Inference production services (hosted API, dedicated pools, private deployments) and covers: rotation transitions, short‑form handoff artifact, escalation timers, evidence collection, postmortem schedule, and customer status / status page templates.

Principles
----------
- SLO first: incidents are triaged against customer‑facing SLOs (availability and latency) and classification is driven by SLO impact.
- Time‑boxed actions: fast containment is prioritized; long investigations are deferred to postmortem.
- Psychological safety: no individual blame; focus on systemic causes and...

title: Controlled Failover Test Results (Game Day) — 2026-01-15
preview:
## Summary
On 2026-01-15 we executed Game Day #1 for the Regional Failover Automation Upgrade. The exercise intentionally impaired **us-east** for a limited set of production traffic and validated our ability to (1) detect regional impairment using the new signal hierarchy, (2) safely shift traffic to **eu-west** us...
text:
## Summary
On 2026-01-15 we executed Game Day #1 for the Regional Failover Automation Upgrade. The exercise intentionally impaired **us-east** for a limited set of production traffic and validated our ability to (1) detect regional impairment using the new signal hierarchy, (2) safely shift traffic to **eu-west** using the failover controller with production guardrails, and (3) recover without oscillation.

**Overall outcome:** Partial success. We met the primary objectives around safe traffic movement and recovery, but identified meaningful gaps in (a) signal corroboration timing, (b) dashboard annotations, and (c) client/streaming behavior during redirects.

## Scope and setup
### Workloads included
- **Hosted API**: limited blast radius using allowlisted tenants + specific routes/models.
- **Dedicated**: one internal test pool + one consenting pilot customer pool in “observe-only” ...

preview:
h1. Summary

text:
h1. Summary
On 2026-01-19 we experienced elevated p95 latency and intermittent HTTP 5xx for a subset of EU-hosted API traffic. The incident lasted 47 minutes end-to-end. Customer impact was concentrated in the eu-west region and primarily affected chat completions with streaming enabled.

*Customer impact:* Yes (degraded performance + partial errors)
*Severity:* SEV-2
*Owning engineering team:* Platform Engineering (Traffic & Edge)
*Primary services:* edge-router, request-gateway, redis-edge-cache

h1. Impact
* ~12% of EU requests exceeded the latency SLO (p95 > 3.5s)
* ~1.8% request failure rate (HTTP 502/503) for EU traffic
* No data loss; retries succeeded after mitigation

h1. Timeline (UTC)
* 10:12 - Alert fired: eu-west request-gateway 5xx > 1%
* 10:15 - On-call acknowledged; triage indicates edge-router cache churn
* 10:21 - Mitigation 1: disabled new cache key format via featu...

title: Incident Seeding and Warm Recovery Playbook
preview:
Overview

Purpose: This playbook explains our controlled incident seeding practice and a standard warm-recovery runloop for LLM-serving incidents. Incident seeding is a deliberate, low-risk exercise where we inject small, observable faults to validate detection, routing, and warm-handoff paths without impacting cust...
text:
Overview

Purpose: This playbook explains our controlled incident seeding practice and a standard warm-recovery runloop for LLM-serving incidents. Incident seeding is a deliberate, low-risk exercise where we inject small, observable faults to validate detection, routing, and warm-handoff paths without impacting customer-facing SLOs. Warm recovery is a set of prioritized, pre-authorized mitigations that restore service quality while preserving user sessions and KV cache state.

Scope
- Services: routing-proxy, runtime-gateway, kv-cache, model-pool, rate-limiter.
- Regions: us-west-2, eu-central-1.
- Tenancy: multitenant hosted API and Dedicated pools.

Definitions
- Seeding event: a controlled fault or degradation introduced for testing (e.g., 20% KV cache eviction, isolated model fallback).
- Warm handoff: transferring active incident ownership from automated systems to oncall with mi...

preview:

Goals:

text:
## Purpose
This page defines the **updated postmortem template (v2)** and the **required classification fields** for all Sev0/Sev1 incidents (and recommended for Sev2+) as part of the Reliability Program: *Incident taxonomy + ownership mapping*.

Goals:
- Ensure every postmortem includes a **standard incident_type/subtype**, a **single accountable owner**, and **consistent contributing systems**.
- Make remediation outcomes measurable: **time-to-mitigate**, **time-to-fix**, and **SLA outcome** by severity and tier (Hosted/Dedicated/Private).
- Improve reporting quality (incident volume, MTTR, SLA breaches) without exploding metric label cardinality.

## What changed (v2)
Postmortems must now include:
1) **Incident taxonomy classification** (type + subtype)
2) **Primary owner team** (single accountable owner)
3) **Contributing systems** (multi-select list; optional contributing taxonom...

title: Postmortem: Autoscaler capacity thrash led to queue saturation (2026-01-09)
preview:
h2. Summary
On 2026-01-09, multiple dedicated pools experienced oscillating capacity (rapid scale up/down) which caused intermittent queue saturation and long wait times for inference requests.

h2. Customer impact
* Time window: 07:4108:33 PT
* Symptoms: throughput drops, queue wait time spikes, intermittent timeou...
text:
h2. Summary
On 2026-01-09, multiple dedicated pools experienced oscillating capacity (rapid scale up/down) which caused intermittent queue saturation and long wait times for inference requests.

h2. Customer impact
* Time window: 07:4108:33 PT
* Symptoms: throughput drops, queue wait time spikes, intermittent timeouts
* Affected: Dedicated customers in us-west using autoscaling policy "balanced"

h2. Detection
* Alert: "dedicated_pool_queue_wait_p95 > 6s"
* CS escalation referencing "autoscaler flapping"

h2. Root cause
A change to the autoscaler cooldown logic reduced the minimum stabilization window from 10 minutes to 2 minutes. Under bursty load, this caused frequent downscales that immediately re-triggered scale-ups, creating a thrash loop and periods of insufficient GPU capacity.

h2. Resolution
* Restored cooldown to 10 minutes
* Temporarily pinned affected pools to a fixed mini...

title: Postmortem: 2025-11-03 Sev1 — Fleet-wide latency cascade triggered by hot /v1/chat/completions route
preview:
## Summary
On 2025-11-03, the Hosted API experienced a Sev1 incident where a bursty, high-token-length workload on the `/v1/chat/completions` route caused a fleet-wide latency cascade across shared GPU pools. The event began as localized p99 latency elevation in `us-east` and progressed to elevated tail latency and ...
text:
## Summary
On 2025-11-03, the Hosted API experienced a Sev1 incident where a bursty, high-token-length workload on the `/v1/chat/completions` route caused a fleet-wide latency cascade across shared GPU pools. The event began as localized p99 latency elevation in `us-east` and progressed to elevated tail latency and increased 5xx rates across multiple routes (chat, embeddings, rerank) due to shared capacity and lack of effective tier-aware admission control.

The primary trigger was a single enterprise tenant workload (large prompts + high concurrency + streaming) that behaved like a noisy neighbor. Our control plane and routing stack at the time did not:
- enforce tier-aware budgets per route,
- gate best-effort traffic early when capacity is constrained,
- export/consume reliable queue depth signals to drive admission decisions,
- provide consistent overload semantics (429s) to shed ...

title: Runbook: Hosted Overload Circuit Breaker (Detection, Mitigation, Rollback, Comms)
preview:
## Purpose
This runbook covers how oncall responds when Redwood Hosted API overload protection (circuit breakers + load shedding) is suspected to be:
1) correctly protecting the platform during real overload, or
2) misfiring (false positives / overly aggressive shedding), or
3) partially degraded (inconsistent Retry...
text:
## Purpose
This runbook covers how oncall responds when Redwood Hosted API overload protection (circuit breakers + load shedding) is suspected to be:
1) correctly protecting the platform during real overload, or
2) misfiring (false positives / overly aggressive shedding), or
3) partially degraded (inconsistent Retry-After, missing correlation IDs, fallback loops).

It includes detection/verification, safe toggles, rollback steps, comms templates, and post-incident data capture.

## Scope
Applies to Redwood Hosted API request paths where overload protection is enforced:
- API Gateway admission control (tenant/route/model caps)
- Smart Routing fallback-on-overload (model/region)
- Serving runtime admission/backpressure signals (queue depth, token/sec saturation, memory pressure proxies)

Out of scope: Dedicated and Private deployments (different controls and SLAs).

- ...

title: Controlled Failover Test Plan (Game Day) — Q1 2026
preview:
## Overview
This document is the run-of-show and safety plan for the Q1 2026 controlled regional failover game day. The goal is to validate Redwood’s end-to-end detection and automated failover behavior (signals → policy evaluation → traffic steering → circuit breakers/load shedding → observability + comms) with a l...
text:
## Overview
This document is the run-of-show and safety plan for the Q1 2026 controlled regional failover game day. The goal is to validate Redwood’s end-to-end detection and automated failover behavior (signals → policy evaluation → traffic steering → circuit breakers/load shedding → observability + comms) with a limited blast radius.

**Primary objective:** Reduce customer-visible impact during a regional impairment by shrinking detection + failover time while preventing cascading overload.

**What we are testing**
- Trigger signal hierarchy and corroboration rules (reachability, 5xx burn, tail latency burn, capacity headroom).
- Deterministic policy evaluation and anti-flap/hold-down behavior.
- Automation controller execution path (including optional approval hooks) and rollback semantics.
- API gateway behavior during failover (timeouts/retry budgets, standardized overload respon...

- Reduce time-to-first-update (TTFU) without sacrificing accuracy.
- Keep language consistent across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private contexts.
- Share useful, actionable info (what’s impacted, where, what c...
text:
## Purpose
This page provides standardized, copy/paste templates for public status page updates. The goals are:
- Reduce time-to-first-update (TTFU) without sacrificing accuracy.
- Keep language consistent across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private contexts.
- Share useful, actionable info (what’s impacted, where, what customers can do) without leaking sensitive details.

These templates are designed for the public status page update field and can be reused in Support macros and Console incident linking.

---

## Audience + tone
**Audience:** customer engineers, support teams, and procurement/security stakeholders reading the public status page.

**Tone principles:**
- Plain language, calm, and factual.
- Use “may be” when uncertain; do not speculate.
- Prefer “We are investigating elevated errors” over “Our database is down.”
- State what’s affected and what’s not affected when known.

**...
